data.title: Fetterman on Democrats: 'Our brand continues to drop'
data.description:
Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) offered a frank assessment in a podcast released Friday about where he believes Democrats’ strengths and challenges lie heading into November’s midterm elections.

Fetterman told Politico’s Dasha Burns on “The Conversation” that he expected Democrats to regain control of the House and narrow the Republican majority in the Senate, suggesting his party’s greatest asset was the “sense of chaos” in domestic politics.

“I’m sure everyone on the Republican side is, you know, planning on that the House will change,” he said, pointing to the potential for Democratic victories in states like North Carolina and Maine.

He noted, however, that Democrats faced a “brand” problem. He pointed to recent polling that indicates the party’s popularity is declining.

“Well, I mean, have you seen the polling?” he asked in response to Burns’s question about what he saw as the “biggest liability” for Democrats. “You know, our brand, it continues to drop.”

A Quinnipiac University poll released last December found that 18 percent of voters approved of the way congressional Democrats were handling their job compared to 73 percent disapproval, a record low rating since the pollster began measuring approval in 2009.

“For me, like that’s … I just want to be the … a Democrat that it’s like hey, I have these values and … and that’s why I vote this way. I don’t treat voters like children where it … it’s like trying to explain why it’s wrong to shut our government down,” he said.

Fetterman, who has said repeatedly that he would never vote to shut the government down, broke with his Senate colleagues on Thursday by voting to advance a full-year DHS funding package – the only Democrat to do so.

He had argued that forcing the agency into a shutdown by stonewalling funding would have little impact on immigration enforcement operations — the main focus of Democrats’ opposition — but harm critical services from the Transportation Security Administration (TSA), the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) and the U.S. Coast Guard.

“If you really want health care, if you really want something, you know, there’s better ways to do this,” he said on the podcast.

Fetterman also bemoaned the rhetoric coming from some members of Congress, specifically criticizing insults and derogatory terms directed at President Trump.

“I’ll always talk and speak, you know, with respect, because I really want to find a way forward to win, for wins in Pennsylvania, and that’s not going to change,” he said.

“And I know some people just want the professional wrestling kind of thing. I know. I know what pays the bills … But for me, I’d rather be just play it straight and treat people on both sides [with] respect, and we’re going to disagree on things that we disagree,” he added.

data.title: US sending world's biggest aircraft carrier to Middle East as tensions with Iran increase
data.description:
The U.S. is sending the world’s largest aircraft carrier to the Middle East, joining the USS Abraham Lincoln and its strike group already positioned in the U.S. Central Command (Centcom) area, as tensions between Washington and Tehran ratchet up. 

The aircraft carrier U.S.S. Gerald R. Ford and its escort ships, which are currently in the Caribbean, are being sent to the Middle East as the Trump administration beefs up its military presence near Iran, according to multiple news outlets.

Centcom referred The Hill to the Pentagon, which declined to comment. The U.S. Navy referred The Hill to U.S. Southern Command (Southcom). 

“While force posture evolves, our operational capability does not. SOUTHCOM forces remain fully ready to project power, defend themselves, and protect U.S. interests in the region,” a Southcom spokesperson said Friday in a statement to The Hill. “At the direction of the President and the Secretary of War, we continue mission-focused operations to counter illicit activities and malign actors in the Western Hemisphere.”

Earlier this week, Trump indicated that he was considering sending another aircraft carrier to the Middle East as he threatened to authorize strikes against Iran. 

“We have an armada that is heading there and another one might be going,” the president said in a Tuesday interview with Axios.

The Gerald R. Ford, which departed Norfolk, Va., in June, was initially sent to a European cruise, but was ordered to head to the Caribbean as the Pentagon was establishing a massive military presence near Venezuela.

The operation led to the successful capture and ouster of the South American nation‘s embattled leader Nicolas Maduro by U.S. personnel on Jan. 3.

The Hill has also reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: Wes Moore says he won't attend governors meeting at White House after dinner snub
data.description:
Maryland Gov. Wes Moore (D) said he will skip the National Governors Association (NGA) meeting at the White House next week after President Trump excluded him and another Democrat from a separate dinner for governors and their families the next day. 

Moore, the vice chair of the bipartisan NGA, addressed the president’s decision to single him out and call him “not worthy” of attending the black-tie dinner at the White House, which is slated to be held the day after a business meeting between all governors and the president. 

“Well, I can’t speak to the president’s heart. I can speak to his actions. And I do want to be clear to the president, respectfully, you do not determine my worthiness. God determines my worthiness. The people of Maryland determine my worthiness. They are who I answer to. Not him,” Moore told CBS News’s Norah O’Donnell in a town hall interview Thursday evening. 

“I have been very clear with the people of my state that I will work with anyone, but I will bow down to no one. And I think the president has a problem with that,” Moore continued, addressing why he thinks the president targeted him specifically. 

The president backed down from an initial decision to invite only Republican governors to what has traditionally been a bipartisan meeting on policy issues at the White House. All Democratic governors on Wednesday afternoon received their invitations to the meeting on Feb. 20.

But Trump doubled down on his decision to exclude Moore and Colorado Gov. Jared Polis (D) from the lavish dinner the following day, slamming the two Democrats in a pair of social media posts.

Moore, in his Thursday interview, said he is prepared to work with the Trump administration to advance the interests of his constituents but will not attend the meeting if he should expect more “name-calling” rhetoric from the president.and similar rhetoric to what Trump 

“But if the point of the meeting — that he’s singled me out and told me I’m disinvited from —… is to turn it into name-calling or to follow what he did in his most recent tweet, which is full of lies and it’s ignorant and it’s unhinged. If the point of the meeting is that, then my answer to the president is very clear: Nah, I’m good,” Moore told O’Donnell.

The veteran journalist asked the governor if that means he won’t attend the meeting, to which all governors are now invited. 

“Oh, I’m good,” Moore responded. “I will not go. Absolutely not.”

data.title: New York Republican asks Supreme Court to restore her congressional district
data.description:
Rep. Nicole Malliotakis (R-N.Y.) is urging the Supreme Court to stop her state judiciary from ordering a redraw of her congressional district ahead of the midterms, casting it as unconstitutional. 

A New York judge struck down Malliotakis’s district’s boundaries for diluting Black and Latino voting strength on Staten Island in violation of the state’s constitution.

“The trial court’s order has thrown New York’s elections into chaos on the eve of the 2026 Congressional Election,” the application states. 

The decision marked a major victory for Democrats, paving the way for them to add a potential pickup seat in the House while throwing Malliotakis’s future into question. 

Her district spans Staten Island and parts of southern Brooklyn. President Trump handily won it, but the lawsuit could push a redraw that links Staten Island with voters in Lower Manhattan. 

The judge’s ruling orders the state’s Independent Redistricting Commission to draw a new map, preventing the midterm contests from moving ahead until then. 

Malliotakis argues it effectively forces the state to racial gerrymander a new district in violation of the federal Constitution’s equal protection guarantee.

The Supreme Court has not yet docketed the application, but Malliotakis’s lawyers said it was filed this week.

Her emergency ask to the justices comes after New York’s top court ruled it had no jurisdiction to immediately intervene. Malliotakis is still pursuing her remaining state court options, but the new emergency appeal warns the election calendar is moving fast. Candidates can begin circulating nominating petitions Feb. 24. 

“While Applicants had hoped—and still hope—that the New York appellate courts put an end to this unconstitutional mischief, they come to this Court now,” the application states. 

The congresswoman asked for a ruling before petitions start circulating to provide certainty. 

By default, the ask will go to Justice Sonia Sotomayor, who handles emergency matters arising from New York. She could act alone or refer it to the full court for a vote, which is typical for major disputes. 

The Supreme Court has already ruled on two emergency appeals involving redistricting efforts ahead of this year’s midterm elections. The justices allowed Texas and California’s new maps to go into effect, which are set to give both major political parties pickup opportunities as part of a mid-decade redistricting arms race.

data.title: Canada and the US still need one another
data.description:
President Trump has announced on his Truth Social platform that he will block the opening of the $4.7 billion Gordon Howe bridge connecting Detroit to Windsor, Ontario unless the U.S. “owns one half of this asset.” In response, Canadian Prime Minister Minister Mark Carney has pointed out that the Canadian Government paid for the bridge, but that the State of Michigan will jointly own the bridge.

Trump has other grievances against Canada, including the sharp drop in Canadian purchases of American whiskey and its miserly defense budgets. But he particularly smolders over Carney’s speech in January at the World Economic Forum, in which the Canadian prime minister stated that in order not to be totally sidelined by the U.S. and China, “the middle powers must act together, because if we’re not at the table, we’re on the menu.” And he added, “This is not sovereignty.”

Carney’s speech may have excited the European members of his audience, but it rang hollow when he promptly signed a new and expansive trade agreement with Beijing. Moreover, the speech did not break any new Canadian ground. To the contrary, it evoked a longstanding and not entirely dormant Canadian international political stance harking back to the years when the Cold War was at its height. Carney reflects a nearly 80-year-old tradition of Canadian leaders who sought to chart a separate path from the U.S.

Lester Pearson was the earliest prominent and vocal  exponent of the notion that his country and other “middle powers” could somehow navigate between the  great powers. Toward the end of World War II, Pearson, the Canadian Ambassador to the U.S., outlined the role of the middle powers, in general and of Canada in particular, with his country “the leader among a group of States which are importing enough to be necessary to the Big Four [the U.S., the United Kingdom, the Soviet Union and China] but not important enough to be accepted as one of that quartet.”

In 1951, Pearson, by then Canada’s External Affairs Minister, told a Canadian audience that there would be “difficulties and frictions” with the U.S. He added that these “will be easier to settle if the United States realizes that while we are most anxious to work with her and support her… we are not willing to be merely an echo of somebody else’s voice.”

Several Canadian leaders shared Pearson’s concerns. Less than a decade later,  Prime Minsiter John Diefenbaker rejected Washington’s plan to place nuclear warheads on the 56 Bomarc missiles that Canada had acquired from the U.S., thereby souring his relationship with President John Kennedy. Moreover, foreshadowing Carney, Diefenbaker tried to reduce his country’s trade deficit with the U.S. and instead diversify trade with different trading partners because he perceived an American threat to Canadian sovereignty.

During the 1960s and early 1970s, Pearson, now Prime Minister, and his successor, Pierre Elliot Trudeau, offered only lukewarm support for the  American War in Vietnam. Three decades later, Prime Minister Jean Chretien openly opposed the American attack on Iraq.

Yet despite the tension with Washington that marked the tenures of all four men, none advocated an open break with the U.S. And neither has Carney.

Carney certainly has reason to resent American bullying. Though he has recently toned down his comments about absorbing Canada into the U.S., Trump could well reiterate his plan at any time. On the other had, Trump’s unhappiness with Canada’s sluggish defense spending and some of its restrictive trade practices is not without merit.

The latest flap over the Gordie Howe bridge is unlikely to be the last between the two countries, certainly not for as long as Donald Trump remains in office.  Nevertheless, both countries will continue to need each other. The U.S. is Canada’s largest trading partner in goods and services, while Canada is America’s second-largest trading partner. The U.S. is the largest investor in Canada;  Canada is the second-largest investor in the U.S. The two countries remain NORAD partners, and jointly operate the North Warning System, whose upgrade I negotiated during the Reagan years,

Carney simply cannot afford a complete rupture with Washington. However much he may resent Trump’s attitude, and however much he seeks to have Canada lead a third way for “middle powers,” he should take care to avoid aggravating the already tense Canadian-American relationship until it reaches a point of no return. That would be a political, military and economic disaster for both countries for which no concert of middle powers could ever compensate.

Dov S. Zakheim is a senior adviser at the Center for Strategic and International Studies and vice chairman of the board for the Foreign Policy Research Institute. He was undersecretary of Defense (comptroller) and chief financial officer for the Department of Defense from 2001 to 2004 and a deputy undersecretary of Defense from 1985 to 1987.

data.title: US business, consumers bore 90 percent of Trump tariff costs: NY Fed
data.description:
U.S. businesses and consumers bore about 90 percent of the cost of President Trump’s sweeping tariffs, according to a recent report by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.

The study, published Thursday, found that the majority of costs that came from tariffs were passed onto the American public in the first 11 months of 2025, contradicting Trump’s promises that foreign companies would pay the import taxes. 

In the first eight months of the year, consumers and businesses were shouldering 94 percent of the economic burden associated with tariffs. The New York Fed noted that tariffs’ pass-through into import prices declined in the latter half of the year, meaning that foreign exporters were taking on a larger share of the tariff incidence. 

The average tariff rate throughout 2025 increased from 2.6 to 13 percent with few dips and spikes midway through the year, according to the report.

“U.S. firms and consumers continue to bear the bulk of the economic burden of the high tariffs imposed in 2025,” the study’s researchers wrote in a blog posted Thursday. 

The report comes as Trump’s tariff regime has received criticism from both foreign adversaries and bipartisan U.S. lawmakers.

In recent weeks, Trump has threatened tariffs on Canada and a number of countries in Europe before later pulling back on the threats. On Wednesday, half a dozen House Republicans were part of the effort to block tariffs Trump promised on Canada. Trump threatened “consequences” on these GOP individuals who voted against him.

“Any Republican, in the House or the Senate, that votes against TARIFFS will seriously suffer the consequences come Election time, and that includes Primaries!” the president wrote in a Truth Social post Wednesday evening.

The New York Fed’s study corroborates the findings of other recent studies which found U.S. businesses and consumers were shouldering more than 90 percent of the economic burden of tariffs.

data.title: One of California's most elusive animals captured near Yosemite
data.description:
MONO COUNTY, Calif. (KSEE/KGPE) — For the first time in years, researchers have captured and collared one of California’s most elusive mammals: the Sierra Nevada red fox.

Fewer than 50 are believed to remain in the Sierra Nevada mountains. Only those who are extremely lucky have caught a glimpse of one at higher elevations near Sonora Pass and Yosemite National Park. 

The rare foxes are considered a “threatened species” in California. They’re a distinct lineage from common red foxes found elsewhere in North America and Europe, says the California Department of Fish and Wildlife.

“According to historical accounts, these alpine red foxes have always been exceedingly rare and elusive,” the Department said.

This one in particular was found in January near Mammoth Lakes, a mountain community south of Yosemite, after 10 years of remote camera monitoring and closely examining scat.

The fox was caught, fitted with a GPS collar and released.

“[This is] a rare and exciting milestone for CDFW biologists,” the California Department of Fish and Wildlife (CDFW) wrote in a post Tuesday. “Encounters like this are incredibly rare and provide invaluable insight for conservation efforts.”

So, how exactly will this help with conservation efforts?

Researchers were also able to put collars on a few of these foxes in 2018 after catching them near Lassen Peak in far northern California. The GPS collars provide real-time information on movement, habitat use, migration, and survival rates.

They say the data “will offer scientists a rare opportunity to better understand the ecology and conservation needs of this remote group.”

A group that, up until 15 years ago, was thought to have disappeared.

data.title: Pilot unharmed after aircraft crashes into trees at Oregon airport, gets stuck
data.description:
PORTLAND, Ore. (KOIN) – A single-occupant plane crashed into some trees at an airport in Oregon this week, stranding the seemingly unhurt pilot high in the air until he could be rescued.

The crash took place on Wednesday at the Wokonda Beach Airport in Waldport, Ore., deputies said.

“The aircraft was reported to be severely damaged, with the pilot still inside, and stuck approximately 50 feet up in the trees,” officials with the Lincoln County Sheriff’s Office wrote on Facebook.

Photos from the Lincoln County Sheriff’s Office show the plane’s pilot, wearing a helmet, standing on the wrecked fuselage of the aircraft as it hung from the trees.

Oregon’s Lincoln Chronicle later reported that the pilot, 80-year-old Les Lacasse, had attempted to take off from the airstrip only moments before the crash, but his Hummel Ultracruiser plane stalled while trying to gain altitude. He was uninjured, according to the outlet.

“The pilot was safely extricated from the aircraft and evaluated by medical staff. No fatalities or injuries resulted in this incident,” Lincoln County deputies said.

The National Transportation Safety Board and the Federal Aviation Administration are investigating the incident. A representative for the FAA was not immediately available to comment on the crash.

On Facebook, commenters were largely impressed that the pilot managed to escape the incident unharmed, with some chalking it up to skill or luck.

“Any landing you can walk away from is a good landing,” one user wrote.

data.title: The normalization of cruelty and bigotry in Trump 2.0
data.description:
Last Dec. 14, police were still investigating the grisly murder of Rob Reiner and his wife — they were stabbed to death in their own home. President Trump, never one to let a tragedy go to waste, took to Truth Social and made it all about himself.

Reiner, Trump posted, was killed “reportedly due to the anger he caused others through his massive, unyielding, and incurable affliction with a mind crippling disease known as TRUMP DERANGEMENT SYNDROME, sometimes referred to as TDS.”

Yes, really. While the Reiners’ son was in custody as the prime suspect, Trump was busy spinning a political revenge theory starring — who else? — Donald Trump. “Insensitive” doesn’t quite cut it. “Cruel” is closer.

You know who really suffers from Trump Derangement Syndrome? Trump, that’s who.

And yet, the public reaction lasted about ten minutes. Democrats were furious; so were some Republicans. But the cycle played out like it always does: Trump says something grotesque, people get mad, and then … we move on. It’s just another day in the American reality show starring Trump.

Fast forward to last week. A blatantly racist video of Barack and Michelle Obama popped up on Truth Social — portraying the Obamas as apes. The post stayed up long enough to generate disgust before Trump blamed a “staffer,” of course refusing to apologize. You’re obviously free to believe his story about the staffer. I don’t.

Again, the backlash came, mostly from Democrats. Some Republicans joined in, but not enough to matter. And again, within days, the story vanished like smoke.

We have become numb to the indecency. It’s just Trump being Trump, right? That’s the excuse. It’s no big deal, his loyal fans tell us. That’s the rationalization. But that normalization — that shrugging acceptance — is the real story. When cruelty and bigotry no longer shock us, something in the culture is broken.

And Trump knows it. In fact, he thrives on it. Outrage isn’t a byproduct of his behavior — it’s the fuel. And now, his newest fixation: putting his name on anything and everything that doesn’t already bear it.

He has already stamped his name on the Kennedy Center, now called — and try not to laugh — “The Donald J. Trump and The John F. Kennedy Memorial Center for the Performing Arts.” Note whose name comes first. Sure, the board made the change. But Trump picked the board.

Next up? He reportedly wants his name on Penn Station and Dulles Airport.

Michelle Cottle of The New York Times put it this way: “Now in his golden years, the president seems determined to put his stamp on public landmarks and institutions built neither by him nor in his honor.” She points out his push to rebrand the U.S. Institute of Peace — even while gutting it — and his obsession with football, including reports he wants his name slapped on the new Washington Commanders stadium.

“A Trump-Dulles rebrand would help him one-up his presidential predecessors, seeing as the international airport is much bigger than nearby Ronald Reagan National Airport,” writes Cottle. “And a Trump Penn Station, in the heart of Manhattan, would rub his name in the faces of all those snooty New Yorkers who continue to deny him the respect he has craved for his entire adult life.”

And just when you think it can’t get any tackier, there’s the golden Trump ballroom going up where the East Wing of the White House used to be, and a 15-foot-tall statue of himself being installed at his Doral golf complex in Florida. The name? “Don Colossus.”

No parody writer could top this.

“People have a God-given right to flush their money down whatever gold-plated toilets they choose,” Cottle says. “Just leave public tax dollars and venerable institutions out of it.”

Maybe that’s why Trump hasn’t tried to slap his name on the Lincoln Memorial or Washington Monument — or put his face up on Mount Rushmore.

Not yet, anyway.

Bernard Goldberg is an Emmy and an Alfred I. duPont-Columbia University award-winning writer and journalist. He is the author of five books and publishes exclusive weekly columns, audio commentaries and Q&As on his Substack page. Follow him @BernardGoldberg.

data.title: Over $1.8M in donations pour in for James Van Der Beek's wife, 6 children
data.description:
AUSTIN (KXAN) – An online fundraiser was started this week following the death of actor James Van Der Beek, 48, who had been in an ongoing battle against cancer, according to an announcement from his family.

A GoFundMe was created on Wednesday by “Friends of the Van Der Beek family,” with a goal of $1.5 million to support the actor’s wife and six children. As of Thursday afternoon, the page had raised over $1.8M.

“Throughout his illness, the family faced not only emotional challenges but also significant financial strain as they did everything possible to support James and provide for his care,” the GoFundMe description reads in part.

“In the wake of this loss, Kimberly and the children are facing an uncertain future,” it reads. “The costs of James’s medical care and the extended fight against cancer have left the family out of funds. They are working hard to stay in their home and to ensure the children can continue their education and maintain some stability during this incredibly difficult time. The support of friends, family, and the wider community will make a world of difference as they navigate the road ahead.”

Van Der Beek’s family announced Wednesday that he’d died that morning after a long battle against cancer. He was diagnosed with Stage 3 colorectal cancer in August 2023.

His family said that the actor “met his final days with courage, faith, and grace.”

The “Dawson’s Creek” star moved from California to Texas in 2020, residing in Spicewood over the last several years. The description said that the money raised would go toward essential living expenses, bills and supporting the children’s education.
